category.name: Tech Decision Trade-offs
category.summary: A reference catalog for consequential engineering and product decisions where context matters more than ideology.

entries.title: Monolith vs Microservices
entries.decisionQuestion: Should this system remain a monolith or be split into microservices?
entries.summary: Usually a team-shape and operational-maturity decision disguised as an architecture preference.
entries.decisionHeuristic: Default to a monolith or modular monolith until service boundaries and release contention are both real.
entries.whatThisIsReallyAbout: Coordination cost, ownership boundaries, deployment independence, and operational burden.
entries.notActuallyAbout: Codebase size or whether the team is modern.
entries.whyItFeelsHard: Both sides have strong narratives, and teams often copy admired companies instead of matching their own stage and constraints.

optionA.name: Monolith
bestWhen: speed of delivery matters more than deployment independence
costs: harder to scale some parts independently
hiddenCosts: can become socially monolithic before technically unmaintainable
teams may defer modularity too long
failureModesWhenMisused: Turns into a dense system with weak boundaries and rising coordination pain.
realWorldFits: an early-stage SaaS product with one main team
an internal business application with tightly connected workflows
a product where most changes still cut across several domains together

optionB.name: Microservices
bestWhen: domain boundaries are real and stable
multiple teams need independent release cadence
platform and observability maturity already exist
strengths: better fault isolation in some cases
hiddenCosts: team communication often becomes the real bottleneck
service boundaries can calcify prematurely
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates distributed complexity without real organizational or domain benefit.
realWorldFits: a mature platform with several teams owning distinct business capabilities
an organization with clear bounded contexts and separate deployment cadence needs
systems where independent scaling and fault isolation are routine concerns

questionsToAsk: Are our domain boundaries real, or are we hoping they become real later?
Is release contention actually hurting us today?
Who will own service operations, tracing, and reliability work?
What would become easier if we split, and what would become harder immediately?
entries.defaultBias: Prefer monolith or modular monolith unless clear pressure justifies service distribution.
antiPatterns: splitting services before ownership boundaries exist
keeping one shared database while pretending services are independent
using service count as a status signal

timeHorizonNotes.optionA: Usually wins early through speed and lower overhead.
timeHorizonNotes.optionB: Wins later only if boundaries, teams, and platform maturity are genuinely ready.

aiCanHelpWith: AI can help map coupling, dependency hotspots, and candidate boundaries.
aiCanMakeWorseBy: AI can make service extraction look cheaper than it is by accelerating code movement without resolving boundary quality.
entries.aiFalseConfidence: Generated service scaffolding looks designed because it uses textbook patterns and compiles cleanly — creating the illusion that domain boundaries have been reasoned about when only code shape has been moved.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: Do not let generated scaffolding substitute for real domain decomposition.

easilyConfusedWith.distinction: That decision is about how to structure code inside one deployment. This one is about whether to split deployments at all.
That decision is about data ownership between services once they exist. This one is about whether they should exist as independent deployments in the first place.
Refactoring preserves deployment topology. This decision changes it — and makes cross-boundary refactoring fundamentally more expensive afterward.

entries.title: Modular Monolith vs Distributed Services
entries.decisionQuestion: Should we enforce boundaries inside one deployable unit or across independently deployed services?
entries.summary: Usually a boundary-quality and deployment-independence trade-off.
entries.decisionHeuristic: If your boundaries are not strong enough inside one runtime, they are usually not strong enough across many.
entries.whatThisIsReallyAbout: Whether the problem is poor modularity or genuine need for runtime/service separation.
entries.notActuallyAbout: Whether the architecture diagram looks more sophisticated.
entries.whyItFeelsHard: Distributed services feel more explicit, but modular monoliths demand stronger internal discipline.

optionA.name: Modular Monolith
bestWhen: boundaries matter but runtime separation is not yet necessary
deployment independence is helpful but not dominant
refactoring across modules is still common
strengths: strong internal boundaries with lower operational overhead
hiddenCosts: internal contracts may be treated casually
teams can pretend modularity exists when it does not
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates a monolith with module-shaped terminology and weak real separation.
realWorldFits: a product with several clear domains but one deployment pipeline
a scale-up that needs stronger boundaries before distribution
a team wanting better separation without service-operations overhead

optionB.name: Distributed Services
strengths: better fault isolation in some cases
hiddenCosts: service separation can freeze poor boundaries
debugging cost grows quickly
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates service count without service clarity.
realWorldFits: a mature product platform with real team autonomy
a system where scaling, isolation, and release independence are economically justified
a business with clear bounded contexts and service operating capability

questionsToAsk: Do we need independent deployment, or just better modularity?
Which pain is dominant today: code coupling or runtime/service coupling?
Who pays the operations cost if we distribute?
Can we describe boundaries clearly without mentioning technology?
entries.defaultBias: Prefer modular monolith until runtime/service separation is clearly justified.
commonBadReasons: services are more scalable by default
antiPatterns: calling packages modules without enforcing boundaries
splitting services while all changes still deploy together
treating runtime distribution as a substitute for design discipline

timeHorizonNotes.optionA: Often wins in the medium term by buying clarity without full distribution cost.
timeHorizonNotes.optionB: Only wins long-term when separation and ownership are both real and durable.

aiCanHelpWith: AI can inspect coupling and propose module/service seams.
aiCanMakeWorseBy: AI can mass-produce service wrappers and APIs without proving runtime separation is needed.
entries.aiFalseConfidence: Generated module scaffolding and pattern-compliant boundaries look like enforcement because the code shape is clean — creating the illusion that module contracts will hold under pressure when only their syntactic surface has been laid down.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: Generated separation is not architectural justification.

easilyConfusedWith.distinction: That decision is about whether to split deployments at all. This one is about how to enforce boundaries inside a single deployable.
That decision is about data ownership across boundaries. This one is about where the boundary line is drawn in the first place.
Reorganizing files preserves coupling. This decision is about whether module boundaries stop hidden coupling from spreading.

entries.title: REST vs GraphQL
entries.decisionQuestion: Should this API surface be REST-based or GraphQL-based?
entries.summary: Usually a consumer-shape and schema-governance decision, not just an API fashion choice.
entries.decisionHeuristic: Use REST by default; move toward GraphQL when consumer variability and aggregation pressure are real and ongoing.
entries.whatThisIsReallyAbout: Consumer flexibility, schema discipline, backend aggregation complexity, and evolution cost.
entries.notActuallyAbout: Which API style is more modern.
entries.whyItFeelsHard: REST feels familiar but sometimes rigid; GraphQL feels elegant but often shifts complexity inward.

optionA.name: REST
costs: backend aggregation often leaks to clients
hiddenCosts: endpoint sprawl can grow quietly
versioning pressure can become awkward
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates fragmented APIs and client orchestration pain.
realWorldFits: public APIs with broad tooling compatibility needs
backend surfaces with relatively stable client shapes

optionB.name: GraphQL
bestWhen: team can manage query behavior and performance
hiddenCosts: backend ownership can blur
schema becomes a product with governance overhead
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates elegant client contracts over messy backend economics.
realWorldFits: multi-client products with different view shapes
frontend-heavy experiences with complex aggregation needs
platforms willing to treat schema governance as a first-class product concern

questionsToAsk: How variable are our consumer data shapes in reality?
Are we solving API design pain or frontend orchestration pain?
Who owns query cost and resolver behavior?
Can we govern schema evolution as a real product surface?
entries.defaultBias: Prefer REST unless consumer flexibility and aggregation pressure clearly justify GraphQL.
antiPatterns: adding GraphQL as a fashion layer over unclear backend ownership
forcing REST when every client reconstructs the same complex view

timeHorizonNotes.optionA: Often wins early and mid-stage through operational simplicity.
timeHorizonNotes.optionB: Wins when schema governance and consumer diversity persist long enough to justify the complexity.

aiCanHelpWith: AI can help map repeated client fetch patterns and schema hotspots.
aiCanMakeWorseBy: AI can generate GraphQL layers quickly, increasing the chance of pretty schema over weak backend discipline.
entries.aiFalseConfidence: Generated GraphQL schemas and REST specs look mature because the syntax is correct and tooling is rich — creating the illusion of well-governed API design when no decision has been made about consumer contracts, versioning, or backend discipline.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: Do not choose GraphQL because AI made it easy to scaffold.

easilyConfusedWith.distinction: That decision is about coupling shape between services. This one is about the shape of a single service's public surface.
That decision is about how to evolve an API over time. This one is about which style you're evolving in the first place.
Choosing OpenAPI vs SDL vs Protobuf is about how to specify contracts. This decision is about the query shape your consumers will actually use.

entries.title: SQL vs NoSQL
entries.decisionQuestion: Should this data model live in a relational or non-relational store?
entries.summary: Usually a query-shape and consistency decision, not a scalability slogan decision.
entries.decisionHeuristic: Default to SQL unless your access patterns, document shape, or scale model clearly reward non-relational storage.
entries.whatThisIsReallyAbout: Access patterns, relational complexity, consistency needs, and operational familiarity.
entries.notActuallyAbout: Which datastore category is more modern or web-scale by default.
entries.whyItFeelsHard: SQL feels structured; NoSQL feels flexible until querying, consistency, and governance mature into pain.

optionA.name: SQL
costs: horizontal scaling can be more complex in some cases
hiddenCosts: bad relational modeling can create performance pain
teams may misuse SQL as an excuse to delay domain design
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates dense relational coupling and poor performance through weak modeling.
realWorldFits: domains with rich joins and transactional integrity requirements

optionB.name: NoSQL
bestWhen: access patterns are simple and well-known
document or key-value shape matches domain reality
strengths: simple scaling models in some workloads
good fit for certain document-centric domains
hiddenCosts: teams often recreate relational needs later
read model convenience can become write model complexity
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates flexible storage with rigid downstream reporting and consistency pain.
realWorldFits: event/session-like data with simple primary access paths

questionsToAsk: What are the real access patterns, not the imagined ones?
Will reporting and ad hoc queries matter later?
What inconsistency can the domain tolerate?
Are we denormalizing because it helps, or because we are avoiding schema thinking?
entries.defaultBias: Prefer SQL unless access patterns and domain shape clearly favor non-relational storage.
commonBadReasons: we do not know schema yet so schema should not exist
antiPatterns: choosing NoSQL because of anticipated scale without query analysis
using SQL for strongly document-shaped access patterns while rebuilding denormalized views everywhere

timeHorizonNotes.optionA: Often wins long-term when query flexibility and reporting become unavoidable.
timeHorizonNotes.optionB: Wins when the access model stays narrow and the workload truly fits it.

aiCanHelpWith: AI can analyze access patterns and generate candidate schema/query comparisons.
aiCanMakeWorseBy: AI can suggest trendy datastore choices detached from real query and consistency needs.
entries.aiFalseConfidence: Generated schemas and sample queries look well-fitted because the code compiles and patterns look idiomatic — creating the illusion that access patterns have been analyzed when only a plausible-looking shape has been picked.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: Do not let generated examples substitute for actual production access-pattern analysis.

easilyConfusedWith.distinction: That decision is about who owns the data. This one is about how the data is modeled and queried regardless of ownership.
That decision is about guarantees on reads. This one is about the store underneath, which may or may not constrain those guarantees.
Choosing RDS vs Dynamo vs Mongo Atlas is about operations. This decision is about the data model, which constrains the provider set but is not the same choice.

entries.title: Sync Communication vs Event-Driven
entries.decisionQuestion: Should services coordinate synchronously or through events?
entries.summary: Usually a coupling, resilience, and process-clarity decision—not just a latency or style choice.
entries.decisionHeuristic: Use sync for short, direct, user-facing flows; use events when decoupling and async process shape are genuinely valuable.
entries.whatThisIsReallyAbout: Temporal coupling, failure handling, business process clarity, and observability maturity.
entries.notActuallyAbout: Which style is more advanced or scalable by default.
entries.whyItFeelsHard: Synchronous flows are easier to reason about directly; event-driven systems reduce coupling but increase coordination complexity.

optionA.name: Synchronous
bestWhen: request-response semantics match the business interaction
failure handling must be simple and explicit
hiddenCosts: downstream fragility can quietly accumulate
availability becomes hostage to dependency behavior
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates brittle dependency chains that make outages and latency spikes propagate quickly.
realWorldFits: user-facing operations where an immediate answer is required

optionB.name: Event-Driven
bestWhen: multiple consumers benefit from emitted facts
team can manage eventual consistency and tracing
hiddenCosts: business flows can become opaque
replay, idempotency, and schema evolution become serious concerns
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates a foggy workflow where events exist but ownership and process clarity do not.
realWorldFits: order lifecycle processing with multiple downstream consumers
systems where work naturally fans out over time

questionsToAsk: Does this workflow truly need an immediate answer?
If one dependency is slow or down, what should happen?
Can we explain the business process clearly if it is asynchronous?
Who owns event contracts, replay, and idempotency?
entries.defaultBias: Prefer sync for simple direct workflows; use events when decoupling and async semantics are genuinely valuable.
antiPatterns: introducing events without clear process ownership
using synchronous chains for workflows that are naturally asynchronous
goodSignalsForB: strong need to reduce temporal coupling

optionB: teams managing event contracts and reconciliation

timeHorizonNotes.optionA: Wins earlier through clarity and simpler control flow.
timeHorizonNotes.optionB: Wins later if the workflow truly benefits from decoupling and the org can sustain the semantics.

aiCanHelpWith: AI can help trace flows, identify coupling hotspots, and explain event chains.
aiCanMakeWorseBy: AI can scaffold brokers, handlers, and consumers quickly, increasing the chance of event-driven complexity without process clarity.
entries.aiFalseConfidence: Generated broker configs, handler stubs, and consumer code look like a working event architecture because they compile and the topology diagrams are clean — creating the illusion that business processes have been mapped when only plumbing has been scaffolded.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: Generated event plumbing does not reduce business ambiguity.

easilyConfusedWith.distinction: That decision is about guarantees after the call finishes. This one is about whether the call is a direct one at all.
That decision is about where data lives. This one is about how services learn that the data changed.
Making an HTTP call asynchronous in code is a concurrency pattern. This decision is whether services coordinate through direct invocation or through events.

entries.title: Shared Database vs Service-Owned Data
entries.decisionQuestion: Should multiple services share one database or own their data separately?
entries.summary: Usually a coupling and boundary-enforcement decision.
entries.decisionHeuristic: If service boundaries are not real enough to own data separately, they are probably not real enough to be services yet.
entries.whatThisIsReallyAbout: Whether data ownership follows service boundaries or cuts across them.
entries.notActuallyAbout: Whether separate databases simply look cleaner on diagrams.
entries.whyItFeelsHard: Shared databases are convenient early; separate ownership is cleaner later but costlier now.

optionA.name: Shared Database
bestWhen: system is still monolithic or near-monolithic
operational simplicity matters more than ownership purity
hiddenCosts: database becomes the real integration surface
changes become politically coupled
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates service names over one shared data reality.

optionB.name: Service-Owned Data
hiddenCosts: teams may underestimate data product/reporting consequences
consistency questions become architectural
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates autonomy theater with reporting pain and unclear truth sources.
realWorldFits: business domains with distinct teams and data stewardship
systems where ownership clarity matters more than ad hoc reporting convenience

questionsToAsk: Where does truth need to live?
Who is allowed to change the shape and meaning of this data?
Are reporting and analytics first-class needs?
Would separate ownership reduce or merely relocate coupling?
entries.defaultBias: Prefer service-owned data only when service boundaries are real enough to sustain it.
commonBadReasons: separate databases always mean better architecture
antiPatterns: sharing tables across services while claiming ownership clarity
splitting data ownership without a plan for reporting and reconciliation

optionA: all teams touching the shared schema

timeHorizonNotes.optionA: Often wins early through simplicity.
timeHorizonNotes.optionB: Wins later only when ownership clarity pays back more than integration/reporting cost.

aiCanHelpWith: AI can trace cross-schema dependencies and ownership violations.
aiCanMakeWorseBy: AI can generate service shells that hide continued database coupling.
entries.aiFalseConfidence: Generated service scaffolding can create the appearance of ownership — separate repos, separate deployments, separate READMEs — while the database stays the real contract underneath, so the system looks decoupled and isn't.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: Generated service boundaries do not matter if the database is still the real contract.

easilyConfusedWith.distinction: That decision is about deployment separation. This one is about whether that separation is meaningful when the database remains shared.
That decision is about guarantees between services. This one is about whether services have separate data at all.
That is about isolating customers. This decision is about isolating services from each other, which is a different separation axis.

entries.title: Strong Consistency vs Eventual Consistency
entries.decisionQuestion: Should this workflow guarantee immediate consistency or tolerate convergence over time?
entries.summary: Usually a business-truth and user-expectation decision, not just a storage decision.
entries.decisionHeuristic: If being temporarily wrong is expensive, default toward strong consistency.
entries.whatThisIsReallyAbout: Whether delayed convergence is acceptable for the domain consequence of being temporarily wrong.
entries.notActuallyAbout: Distributed systems vocabulary for its own sake.
entries.whyItFeelsHard: Strong consistency feels safer; eventual consistency feels more scalable and decoupled, until user confusion or business error appears.

optionA.name: Strong Consistency
bestWhen: financial or business correctness is critical immediately
double-spend or conflicting action risk is high
hiddenCosts: can push teams toward over-centralization
availability trade-offs may grow
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates rigid coordination where looser semantics would have been enough.
realWorldFits: inventory reservation with strong conflict cost

optionB.name: Eventual Consistency
hiddenCosts: teams often under-design compensations and user communication
operational debugging gets harder
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates business ambiguity disguised as technical flexibility.
realWorldFits: systems where the user can tolerate slightly stale views

questionsToAsk: What happens if two systems disagree for 5 seconds, 5 minutes, or 1 hour?
Would users see this as a bug or an acceptable delay?
Can we explain the anomaly and recover cleanly?
What compensations are required if we are eventually consistent?
entries.defaultBias: Prefer strong consistency where being temporarily wrong is expensive; use eventual consistency where delay is acceptable and modeled explicitly.
commonBadReasons: eventual consistency is what distributed systems do
strong consistency is always too expensive
antiPatterns: claiming eventual consistency without defining user-visible consequences
using strong consistency for low-value workflows that do not need it
goodSignalsForA: money or inventory correctness matters immediately
goodSignalsForB: temporary lag is acceptable and explainable

timeHorizonNotes.optionA: Often wins whenever trust and correctness are central.
timeHorizonNotes.optionB: Wins when workflow elasticity and decoupling matter more than immediate truth.

aiCanHelpWith: AI can help simulate edge cases and identify compensation paths.
aiCanMakeWorseBy: AI can generate architecture proposals that invoke eventual consistency without modeling business reconciliation.
entries.aiFalseConfidence: AI-generated architecture proposals can invoke eventual consistency as a best-practice keyword and make the system sound modern, hiding the fact that no one has modeled the business reconciliation that eventual consistency requires.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: Do not let architectural vocabulary outrun business semantics.

easilyConfusedWith.distinction: That decision is about how services coordinate. This one is about what users and business processes see once coordination has happened.
That decision is about the storage engine. This one is about guarantees on reads, which some engines support and others don't.
ACID/BASE is the vendor vocabulary. This decision is about what your users and reconciliation processes can actually tolerate.

entries.title: Batch vs Real-Time Processing
entries.decisionQuestion: Should this data or workflow be processed in scheduled batches or in real time?
entries.summary: Usually a freshness-vs-complexity decision.
entries.decisionHeuristic: Prefer batch unless faster freshness clearly changes business value.
entries.whatThisIsReallyAbout: How much latency the business actually needs versus how much operational complexity it can afford.
entries.notActuallyAbout: Whether the system sounds more modern or advanced.
entries.whyItFeelsHard: Real time sounds better; batch is often enough and much simpler.

optionA.name: Batch
bestWhen: freshness requirements are measured in minutes or hours
hiddenCosts: batch windows can become implicit deadlines
large failure recovery can be painful
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates stale systems where timeliness actually matters.

optionB.name: Real Time
bestWhen: freshness directly affects user value or risk
strengths: more immediate user or business impact
hiddenCosts: real-time pipelines can be expensive to operate for marginal business gain
downstream systems may not actually be real-time ready
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates expensive always-on pipelines without meaningful business leverage.
realWorldFits: user-facing workflow state that must update immediately

questionsToAsk: What changes if this is 1 second late, 1 minute late, or 1 hour late?
Who truly benefits from freshness?
Can downstream consumers actually use real-time data?
How expensive is reprocessing and recovery?
entries.defaultBias: Prefer batch unless real-time freshness is materially valuable.
antiPatterns: building streaming pipelines for dashboards nobody watches live
keeping batch pipelines where user-facing harm from staleness is already clear
goodSignalsForB: freshness directly changes user or business value

optionA: business stakeholders waiting for slower data

timeHorizonNotes.optionA: Often wins longer than teams expect because simpler systems stay reliable.
timeHorizonNotes.optionB: Wins when freshness is genuinely monetized or risk-relevant.

aiCanHelpWith: AI can help estimate freshness value versus complexity burden.
aiCanMakeWorseBy: AI may recommend streaming or real-time designs as generic best practice.
entries.aiFalseConfidence: Real-time architectures sound more modern in a generated design doc because the vocabulary is on-trend, creating the illusion of a latency win that no user will ever feel.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: Do not optimize latency nobody will feel.

easilyConfusedWith.distinction: That decision is about inter-service coordination. This one is about how fresh the processed result is.
Choosing Kafka Streams vs Flink vs Spark Streaming is about the engine. This decision is whether streaming is the right mode at all.
Scheduling cadence is how often batches run. This decision is whether it should be a batch at all.

entries.title: Edge Logic vs Centralized Backend Logic
entries.decisionQuestion: Should logic execute closer to users or requests at the edge, or remain centralized in backend systems?
entries.summary: Usually a latency-and-distribution decision with governance consequences.
entries.decisionHeuristic: Keep logic centralized unless edge placement creates a clear latency or locality win and the logic remains governable.
entries.whatThisIsReallyAbout: Where logic should live for performance, control, observability, and consistency.
entries.notActuallyAbout: Whether edge runtimes are inherently superior architecture.
entries.whyItFeelsHard: Edge execution can improve responsiveness, but it spreads logic into a harder-to-govern surface.

optionA.name: Edge Logic
bestWhen: request shaping or local decisions are lightweight
hiddenCosts: logic can drift between edge and core systems
observability may lag behind execution spread
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates fragmented logic with weak traceability.

optionB.name: Centralized Backend Logic
bestWhen: behavior consistency matters more than latency wins
hiddenCosts: teams may over-centralize simple decisions that could be local
global user experience may suffer
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates unnecessarily centralized systems with weak local responsiveness.
realWorldFits: complex domain workflows where central truth matters more than milliseconds

questionsToAsk: Which logic truly benefits from being near the user?
How will we observe and audit behavior if it runs at the edge?
Can the same rule accidentally exist in both edge and backend layers?
What is the consequence of inconsistent behavior across regions or runtimes?
entries.defaultBias: Prefer centralized logic unless edge placement yields real user or system benefit and governance remains manageable.
commonBadReasons: all logic should live as close to user as possible
antiPatterns: duplicating the same rule at edge and backend
moving policy-heavy logic to the edge for prestige rather than value

timeHorizonNotes.optionA: Wins when low-latency locality remains a durable product advantage.
timeHorizonNotes.optionB: Wins when coherence, policy consistency, and observability dominate.

aiCanHelpWith: AI can map duplicated logic between edge and core systems.
aiCanMakeWorseBy: AI can scaffold edge functions quickly, increasing logic sprawl risk.
entries.aiFalseConfidence: Generated edge functions look like architecture because they deploy cleanly and return fast — creating the illusion of owned logic when nobody has inventoried where it runs, who owns it, or how it's observed once spread across regions.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: Generated edge code increases sprawl if ownership is weak.

easilyConfusedWith.distinction: That decision is about when data is processed. This one is about where logic executes relative to the request.
That decision is about team structure. This one is about runtime structure, but similar governance risks apply when logic sprawls across locations.
Caching serves static content close to users. This decision is about running logic close to users, which is a different risk surface.

entries.title: Build vs Buy
entries.decisionQuestion: Should we build this capability ourselves or buy an external solution?
entries.summary: Usually a control-vs-focus decision, not an engineering pride decision.
entries.decisionHeuristic: Buy unless the capability is core, differentiating, or structurally awkward to outsource.
entries.whatThisIsReallyAbout: Differentiation, ownership burden, integration cost, and long-term leverage.
entries.notActuallyAbout: Whether the team is capable or proud enough to build it.
entries.whyItFeelsHard: Buying feels like loss of control; building feels like hidden commitment disguised as freedom.

optionA.name: Build
hiddenCosts: support, compliance, security, and operational responsibilities accumulate
internal products often outlive enthusiasm
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates bespoke infrastructure or product surface that is expensive to maintain and not strategically valuable.
realWorldFits: cases where vendor shape would fight the product shape constantly

optionB.name: Buy
hiddenCosts: integration glue can quietly become your real product
switching cost can become political and technical
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates a vendor-shaped architecture with weak internal leverage and painful lock-in.
realWorldFits: standard business capabilities like analytics, ticketing, or auth-adjacent surfaces
areas where speed-to-value matters more than deep customization

questionsToAsk: Is this genuinely differentiating, or just important?
Who will maintain this in three years?
What happens if the vendor changes pricing, roadmap, or behavior?
What is the real integration cost, not the brochure integration cost?
entries.defaultBias: Buy unless the capability is core, differentiating, or structurally hard to externalize.
commonBadReasons: building once is cheaper than paying subscription
antiPatterns: building commodity capability because engineers dislike vendors
buying a tool and then recreating half its product internally

optionB: future migration team if lock-in grows

timeHorizonNotes.optionA: May win long-term if the capability is truly core and durable.
timeHorizonNotes.optionB: Usually wins short to medium term if the capability is commodity and integration is bounded.

aiCanHelpWith: AI can accelerate vendor evaluation, integration mapping, and internal prototype comparison.
aiCanMakeWorseBy: AI lowers the apparent cost of building prototypes, making teams underestimate lifecycle ownership.
entries.aiFalseConfidence: Generated prototypes reach the 'looks working' stage in hours, creating the illusion that owning the full lifecycle — hardening, integration, operations, support — will be similarly cheap.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: A fast AI-generated prototype is not evidence that owning the full product is cheap.

easilyConfusedWith.distinction: That decision is about the shape of the solution you own. This decision is about whether you own it at all.
Platform-first is about where a capability lives inside your org. Build-vs-buy is about whether that capability comes from inside at all.
An integration decision is how to glue your system to a third-party product. Build-vs-buy is whether to rely on a third-party product in the first place.

entries.title: Speed vs Robustness
entries.decisionQuestion: Should we optimize for faster delivery now or for stronger resilience and hardening now?
entries.summary: Usually a risk-timing trade-off, not a values trade-off.
entries.decisionHeuristic: Move fast where rollback is cheap; harden early where trust and consequence are expensive.
entries.whatThisIsReallyAbout: When to ship earlier versus when fragility cost is too high.
entries.notActuallyAbout: Whether the team cares about quality.
entries.whyItFeelsHard: Everyone wants both, but the timeline rarely funds both fully at the same moment.

optionA.name: Speed
hiddenCosts: temporary shortcuts often become inherited defaults
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates synthetic velocity and hidden fragility.

optionB.name: Robustness
costs: more upfront design and testing effort
hiddenCosts: teams may over-harden before validating value
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates overbuilt systems that solve risks nobody is yet facing.

questionsToAsk: What happens if this breaks in production tomorrow?
Can we roll back cleanly?
What learning do we gain by shipping sooner?
Who pays the cost if we under-harden?
entries.defaultBias: Bias toward speed where reversibility is high; bias toward robustness where consequence is large.
antiPatterns: calling recklessness speed
over-hardening early product uncertainty into premature complexity

timeHorizonNotes.optionA: Wins early when uncertainty is high and rollback is cheap.
timeHorizonNotes.optionB: Wins wherever trust, scale, or irreversibility make failure expensive.

aiCanHelpWith: AI can help accelerate testing and review, partially reducing the trade-off.
aiCanMakeWorseBy: AI can make speed look safer than it is by increasing output without proportionate hardening.
entries.aiFalseConfidence: AI raises output speed without a proportionate increase in hardening practice, so 'we're shipping faster' looks like improved delivery when it's actually a larger volume moving through the same unchanged robustness discipline.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: Faster generation does not eliminate robustness work.

easilyConfusedWith.distinction: That decision is about the gate in CI. This one is about the timing of hardening across the whole lifecycle.
That decision is about the system's willingness to change. This one is about the speed/hardening trade-off at the feature level.
Prioritization is what to work on. This decision is how much hardening to put into whatever you chose.

entries.title: Scope Flexibility vs Date Certainty
entries.decisionQuestion: Should scope move to preserve date, or should date move to preserve scope?
entries.summary: Usually a commitment-shape decision, not a planning-format decision.
entries.decisionHeuristic: Be explicit: one of date, scope, or quality must be more flexible.
entries.whatThisIsReallyAbout: Which constraint is genuinely fixed and whether everyone agrees on that reality.
entries.notActuallyAbout: How well the team estimates in the abstract.
entries.whyItFeelsHard: Teams often speak as though both date and scope are negotiable until late panic reveals one was not.

optionA.name: Scope Flexibility
costs: stakeholder disappointment if not handled well
quality can still be silently traded if discipline is weak
hiddenCosts: scope cuts can become accidental product drift
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates a stealthy degradation of value under fixed calendar pressure.
realWorldFits: milestones where partial value still matters

optionB.name: Date Flexibility
bestWhen: scope integrity matters more than fixed timing
coordination cost of partial release is high
hiddenCosts: dates may slip repeatedly if value shaping stays weak
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates endless extension with no real finish discipline.
realWorldFits: products where a partial version would create more confusion than value

questionsToAsk: Which constraint is truly fixed?
Can we cut scope without cutting coherence?
What does success look like if we ship partial value?
Are we protecting scope, date, or ego?
entries.defaultBias: Explicitly choose the fixed constraint rather than pretending all three are fixed.
commonBadReasons: we can preserve both if the team pushes harder
antiPatterns: pretending scope and date are both fixed until quality absorbs the truth
reducing scope without redefining success criteria
goodSignalsForB: date is internally rather than externally fixed

timeHorizonNotes.optionA: Wins when timing creates most of the value.
timeHorizonNotes.optionB: Wins when coherence of the delivered whole matters more than calendar precision.

aiCanHelpWith: AI can help scenario-plan different scope and date shapes.
aiCanMakeWorseBy: AI can hide schedule risk by making reports look calmer and output look faster.
entries.aiFalseConfidence: AI-polished status updates read as measured and under control, creating the illusion that the commitment is well-managed when the underlying scope/date/quality trade-off has not actually been made.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: A polished summary is not an actual constraint decision.

easilyConfusedWith.distinction: That decision is about reversibility of a specific change. This one is about which commitment axis (scope or date) you're willing to move when reality disagrees with the plan.
That decision is about how much hardening to invest in. This one is about which commitment moves when reality doesn't match the plan.
Resetting the deadline is an outcome. This decision is the framing choice that determines whether it ever happens.

entries.title: Platform First vs Product First
entries.decisionQuestion: Should we invest in a generalized internal platform now or solve immediate product needs first?
entries.summary: Usually a leverage-timing decision, not a maturity decision.
entries.decisionHeuristic: Product-first until repeated shared demand is proven; platform-first only when real adopters already exist.
entries.whatThisIsReallyAbout: When reuse is real, when it is imaginary, and how much evidence should exist before internal platform investment.
entries.notActuallyAbout: Whether platform work sounds more strategic.
entries.whyItFeelsHard: Platform work feels strategic and clean; product-first work feels messier but often more grounded.

optionA.name: Platform First
bestWhen: multiple teams are blocked by the same capability gap
adoption demand is real and measurable
hiddenCosts: platform can become prestige work without real users
teams may be forced into premature standardization
failureModesWhenMisused: Becomes platform-before-product: elegant internal infrastructure waiting for a real problem.
realWorldFits: internal developer platform with proven consumer demand
shared deployment/runtime capability needed by many teams now
well-understood repeated workflow pain across the organization

optionB.name: Product First
bestWhen: user value is not yet well validated
speed of delivery matters more than generalized leverage
hiddenCosts: teams may postpone shared investment too long
local solutions can harden
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates repeated local hacks that later resist consolidation.
realWorldFits: single-team initiatives with unclear future reuse
domains where actual usage patterns are still emerging

questionsToAsk: Who are the real adopters right now?
What repeated pain does the platform remove today?
Would solving the product problem first teach us something critical?
Is the platform a product with users, or a hope with diagrams?
entries.defaultBias: Prefer product-first until repeated shared need is proven.
commonBadReasons: we should build reusable foundations first
duplication must always be eliminated immediately
antiPatterns: building a platform with no real consumers
repeating local hacks long after shared pain is obvious

timeHorizonNotes.optionA: Wins when repeated demand is already real enough to pay back shared investment.
timeHorizonNotes.optionB: Wins when the domain still needs to teach you what the reusable problem actually is.

aiCanHelpWith: AI can help compare repeated patterns across teams and reveal whether the platform case is real.
aiCanMakeWorseBy: AI makes internal platform scaffolding cheap, increasing the temptation to build elegant but weakly demanded foundations.
entries.aiFalseConfidence: Generated platform scaffolding is cheap, so a usable-looking internal platform appears quickly — creating the illusion of leverage when no product team has adopted it under real conditions.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: A fast platform prototype is not evidence of durable adoption demand.

easilyConfusedWith.distinction: That decision is about whether to own a capability at all. This one is about whether to invest in generalized internal capability before a specific product need has proved it out.
That decision assumes a platform exists and asks who owns it. This one is whether to build one before demand is proven.
That decision is about a single solution's shape. This one is about an org-wide investment timing.

entries.title: Rewrite vs Refactor
entries.decisionQuestion: Should we replace this system wholesale or improve it incrementally?
entries.summary: Usually a migration-discipline decision, not a cleanliness decision.
entries.decisionHeuristic: Refactor by default; rewrite only when migration slices and retirement logic are explicit.
entries.whatThisIsReallyAbout: Risk containment, value continuity, and how much hidden system behavior still matters.
entries.notActuallyAbout: Whether the old code looks ugly or embarrassing.
entries.whyItFeelsHard: Refactoring feels slow and compromised; rewriting feels clean but often hides displacement risk.

optionA.name: Rewrite
bestWhen: legacy behavior can be mapped reliably
costs: longer path to equivalent business value
hiddenCosts: unknown legacy behavior surfaces late
teams often add ambition while rewriting
failureModesWhenMisused: Turns into the friendly rewrite: a prestige effort with weak cutover discipline.
realWorldFits: systems with irrecoverable foundational constraints and a credible migration plan
small replaceable internal tools with bounded behavior
surfaces where the legacy workload can be retired slice by slice

optionB.name: Refactor
bestWhen: delivery pressure cannot tolerate parallel rebuild
strengths: better learning from real system behavior
hiddenCosts: without discipline, refactoring can become endless patching
teams may lose morale if progress feels invisible
failureModesWhenMisused: Becomes endless compromise without meaningful structural improvement.
realWorldFits: core business systems that must keep shipping
products with valuable but messy codebases
systems where seams for incremental change already exist

questionsToAsk: What does the old system do that we do not fully understand yet?
Can we name the first slice to replace or improve?
What is the cutover and retirement plan?
Are we trying to fix pain or buy hope?
entries.defaultBias: Prefer refactor unless rewrite has explicit migration slices and retirement logic.
commonBadReasons: rewriting will be faster than understanding the old system
antiPatterns: starting a rewrite without parity criteria
calling endless patching refactoring
mixing migration with major redesign ambition
goodSignalsForA: migration can be sliced and measured

optionB: delivery timelines through slower visible change

timeHorizonNotes.optionA: May win only if the new target is genuinely better and retirement is believable.
timeHorizonNotes.optionB: Usually wins by preserving continuity while paying down risk incrementally.

aiCanHelpWith: AI can help uncover legacy behavior and identify refactor seams.
aiCanMakeWorseBy: AI makes rewriting feel faster by making code generation cheaper, but it does not reduce hidden behavior risk.
entries.aiFalseConfidence: Generated replacement code compiles and passes shallow tests, making a rewrite feel fast and de-risked — creating the illusion that replacement behavior has been validated when only replacement shape has been laid down.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: Generated replacement code does not equal validated replacement behavior.

easilyConfusedWith.distinction: That decision is about deployment topology. This one is about whether to keep evolving the current code at all.
That decision is about commitment axes. This one is about the path to the same outcome (replacement vs incremental improvement).
Modernization is a framing. This decision is which of its two concrete forms to take.

entries.title: Generic Solution vs Case-Specific Solution
entries.decisionQuestion: Should we build for broad reuse now or solve this specific case directly?
entries.summary: Usually an abstraction-timing decision.
entries.decisionHeuristic: Solve the specific case first unless you already have multiple real consumers.
entries.whatThisIsReallyAbout: Whether reuse is real yet or merely hoped for.
entries.notActuallyAbout: Whether genericity sounds smarter than practicality.
entries.whyItFeelsHard: Generic work feels strategic; case-specific work feels humble but often truer.

optionA.name: Generic Solution
hiddenCosts: genericity can outrun actual demand
edge cases can dominate design too early
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates abstraction addiction and platform-before-product dynamics.
realWorldFits: internal frameworks with several real teams depending on them
repeated workflow tooling with known, stable variance
common infrastructure components with proven shared demand

optionB.name: Case-Specific Solution
bestWhen: speed matters more than speculative reuse
hiddenCosts: shortcuts can harden into defaults
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates scattered local fixes that later resist generalization.
realWorldFits: first implementations of a new workflow
bounded internal tools solving one near-term pain

questionsToAsk: Who else needs this today, not hypothetically?
What variation do we actually understand?
If we make this generic now, who will pay the complexity cost?
What would we lose by solving the specific case first?
entries.defaultBias: Solve the specific case first unless repeated demand is already proven.
whenToRevisit: a second and third real consumer emerge
antiPatterns: building a framework before the second real consumer exists
rebuilding similar things repeatedly while pretending no pattern exists

optionA: future consumers if complexity leaks outward

timeHorizonNotes.optionA: Wins when reuse is already real enough to justify upfront complexity.
timeHorizonNotes.optionB: Wins early by preserving clarity and letting the real pattern emerge.

aiCanHelpWith: AI can compare multiple implementations to discover whether a true pattern exists.
aiCanMakeWorseBy: AI makes generic scaffolding cheap, increasing the temptation to generalize too early.
entries.aiFalseConfidence: Generated generic scaffolding looks reusable because the interfaces are polished and the patterns are on-trend — creating the illusion of leverage when no second use case has actually validated the abstraction.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: Generated genericity is still complexity.

easilyConfusedWith.distinction: That decision is about org-level investment. This one is about the shape of a specific solution.
That decision is about optionality inside a solution. This one is about whether the solution covers multiple cases at all.
Extracting a helper is a local refactor. This decision is a lifecycle commitment to serve more than one concrete case.

entries.title: One-Way Door vs Two-Way Door Rollout
entries.decisionQuestion: Is this change hard to reverse, or can we rollout in a reversible way?
entries.summary: Usually a reversibility and blast-radius decision.
entries.decisionHeuristic: If uncertainty is high and rollback is feasible, prefer a two-way door.
entries.whatThisIsReallyAbout: How much safety comes from keeping options open versus committing fully.
entries.notActuallyAbout: Whether confidence is high in a meeting.
entries.whyItFeelsHard: Irreversible changes can be cleaner and faster; reversible changes are safer but often more complex.

optionA.name: One-Way Door
bestWhen: reversal cost is acceptable only because confidence is high
dual-running or gradual fallback is impractical
hiddenCosts: organizations often overestimate certainty
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates abrupt, high-blast-radius transitions.
realWorldFits: schema migrations with no dual-running path
sunsetting legacy platforms after explicit readiness criteria

optionB.name: Two-Way Door
bestWhen: gradual migration or flags are feasible
hiddenCosts: temporary rollback paths can become permanent clutter
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates long-lived transitional complexity nobody retires.

questionsToAsk: What would rollback actually require in practice?
Have we rehearsed the reversal path?
How much uncertainty still exists?
What temporary complexity are we willing to tolerate for safety?
entries.defaultBias: Prefer two-way doors when uncertainty is high and rollback is feasible.
commonBadReasons: we will know if it fails
antiPatterns: calling a change reversible without testing rollback
leaving temporary rollback paths in place forever

timeHorizonNotes.optionA: Wins when certainty is unusually high and transitional complexity is too costly.
timeHorizonNotes.optionB: Wins when learning under controlled risk is more valuable than clean-looking commitment.

aiCanHelpWith: AI can help enumerate rollback paths and rollout hazards.
aiCanMakeWorseBy: AI-generated rollout scripts can create false confidence in reversibility.
entries.aiFalseConfidence: AI-generated rollback scripts, runbooks, and playbooks create the illusion of reversibility — a scripted rollback is not the same as a proven rollback, and a change can still be effectively one-way even when it looks reversible on paper.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: Scripted rollback is not the same as proven rollback.

easilyConfusedWith.distinction: That decision is about commitment axes at the plan level. This one is about reversibility at the change level.
That decision is about the mechanism for controlled release. This one is about how reversible the underlying change is regardless of mechanism.
A rollback plan is the artifact. This decision is whether reversibility is actually achievable.

entries.title: Customization vs Standardization
entries.decisionQuestion: Should we adapt the system heavily for local needs or push toward standard patterns?
entries.summary: Usually a local-fit vs long-term coherence decision.
entries.decisionHeuristic: Standardize unless local variation creates real differentiated value.
entries.whatThisIsReallyAbout: How much local variation the organization can afford to own.
entries.notActuallyAbout: Whether every stakeholder preference deserves product complexity.
entries.whyItFeelsHard: Customization wins immediate satisfaction; standardization wins future manageability.

optionA.name: Customization
bestWhen: customer or domain value depends on variation
hiddenCosts: variation multiplies support and testing burdens
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates fragmented systems with weak coherence.
realWorldFits: enterprise products with genuinely distinct customer operating models
multi-country behavior where legal rules differ materially

optionB.name: Standardization
bestWhen: variation is mostly preference rather than necessity
hiddenCosts: rigid standardization can ignore real value-creating differences
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates systems that are simple to operate but weakly matched to real users.
realWorldFits: products where consistency is itself part of the value
shared operating workflows with low true variance

questionsToAsk: Is this variation a need or a preference?
Who pays the lifetime cost of each customization?
How many variants can we realistically test and support?
Does standardization destroy value, or just reduce noise?
entries.defaultBias: Standardize unless local variation creates real differentiated value.
antiPatterns: adding customer-specific behavior for preferences rather than needs
using standardization as an excuse to ignore real regulatory or domain differences
goodSignalsForB: variation mostly changes presentation or preference

optionB: sales or implementation teams if fit is too rigid

timeHorizonNotes.optionA: May win in specific markets or regulated contexts where local fit creates durable value.
timeHorizonNotes.optionB: Usually wins long-term by preserving coherence and lowering support complexity.

aiCanHelpWith: AI can help cluster variation requests into standardizable patterns.
aiCanMakeWorseBy: AI can make local customization cheaper, risking customization sprawl.
entries.aiFalseConfidence: Cheap AI-generated local variants make customization look like a harmless adaptation choice — each variant looks small, but the aggregate lifecycle complexity is not visible until the fleet is already inconsistent.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: Cheaper customization does not remove lifecycle complexity.

easilyConfusedWith.distinction: That decision is about optionality inside a single product. This one is about variation across instances or customers.
That decision is about team authority. This one is about product/system variance, though the two often move together.
Multi-tenancy is about isolation. This decision is about per-tenant variation, which is a separate axis.

entries.title: Experimentation vs Operational Stability
entries.decisionQuestion: How much change and experimentation can this system tolerate without undermining reliability?
entries.summary: Usually a learning-speed vs trust-preservation decision.
entries.decisionHeuristic: Experiment at the edges and protect trust-critical cores.
entries.whatThisIsReallyAbout: Where experimentation belongs and how much operational surface should be exposed to it.
entries.notActuallyAbout: Whether innovation and reliability are enemies.
entries.whyItFeelsHard: Experimentation drives learning; stability preserves trust. Both are necessary but unevenly by surface.

optionA.name: Experimentation
hiddenCosts: teams may experiment on the wrong surfaces
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates trust erosion under a banner of learning.

optionB.name: Operational Stability
bestWhen: learning gain is low relative to risk
hiddenCosts: teams may become overly change-averse
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates safe stagnation where experimentation could have been low risk and high value.
realWorldFits: regulated workflows where variance carries major consequence

questionsToAsk: What do we learn by experimenting here?
What trust do we risk if we are wrong?
Can we move the experiment to a safer surface?
Is the learning worth the operational cost?
entries.defaultBias: Experiment at edges and low-risk surfaces; protect trust-critical cores.
antiPatterns: running experiments on trust-critical paths because the tooling makes it easy
treating stability as a reason to avoid all learning
goodSignalsForA: high uncertainty and high learning value

timeHorizonNotes.optionA: Wins where rapid learning compounds product advantage.
timeHorizonNotes.optionB: Wins where preserving trust creates more durable value than experimentation velocity.

aiCanHelpWith: AI can simulate scenarios and narrow where live experimentation is actually needed.
aiCanMakeWorseBy: AI lowers cost of trying more things, increasing temptation to experiment without enough operational guardrails.
entries.aiFalseConfidence: Lower cost of change from AI tooling makes experimentation feel inherently safe, but the underlying operational risk (blast radius, detection, rollback) has not changed — so cheap experimentation is not the same as safe experimentation.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: Lower cost of change increases governance need, not decreases it.

easilyConfusedWith.distinction: That decision is about feature hardening timing. This one is about system willingness to be changed at all.
That decision is about workflow design. This one is about change cadence at the system level.
A/B testing is the mechanism. This decision is about the system's tolerance for running experiments at all.

entries.title: Central Platform Team vs Embedded Enablement
entries.decisionQuestion: Should platform capability live in a central team or be embedded to enable product teams directly?
entries.summary: Usually a leverage-distribution decision.
entries.decisionHeuristic: Keep a central platform core; embed selectively where adoption or context gaps are the real bottleneck.
entries.whatThisIsReallyAbout: How platform expertise scales without becoming a bottleneck or disappearing into every team unevenly.
entries.notActuallyAbout: Whether platform work should feel centralized or collaborative as an identity question.
entries.whyItFeelsHard: Central teams create consistency; embedding creates proximity. Both can fail on different axes.

optionA.name: Central Platform Team
hiddenCosts: platform can optimize for itself over adopters
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates ticket-based dependency on a remote internal vendor.

optionB.name: Embedded Enablement
hiddenCosts: enablement people can become roaming heroes rather than capability builders
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates dependency on individuals and inconsistent practices.
realWorldFits: rolling out a platform to product teams with low adoption maturity
temporary embedded reliability or developer productivity support

questionsToAsk: Is the current bottleneck standards, or adoption?
Who owns the platform as a product?
Are teams blocked by missing capability or by missing support and translation?
How do we avoid embedding becoming hero-dependent?
entries.defaultBias: Use a central platform core, embed selectively where adoption or context needs justify it.
antiPatterns: central platform acting like an internal ticket desk
embedded enablement without a path back to shared capability
goodSignalsForA: platform can behave like a product
goodSignalsForB: adoption is failing due to context gap

timeHorizonNotes.optionA: Wins when shared leverage and standards are the main constraint.
timeHorizonNotes.optionB: Wins as a tactical pattern when context and adoption friction dominate.

aiCanHelpWith: AI can document platform usage patterns and common friction points.
aiCanMakeWorseBy: AI can accelerate platform artifact production without proving adoption quality.
entries.aiFalseConfidence: Generated platform docs, templates, and tooling scaffolding make the platform look adopted because the artifacts are polished — when the only thing that scaled was production of the artifacts, not the teams using them.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: Platform polish is not platform usefulness.

easilyConfusedWith.distinction: That decision is about whether a platform should exist at all. This one assumes it does and asks how the people doing platform work should relate to product teams.
That decision is about team composition inside any unit. This one is specifically about the relationship between platform producers and platform users.
A staffing decision sets how many people work on platform. This decision sets the ownership model they work under once they are there.

entries.title: Specialist Teams vs Cross-Functional Teams
entries.decisionQuestion: Should work be organized around specialists or end-to-end cross-functional teams?
entries.summary: Usually a coordination-vs-depth decision, not a modernity decision.
entries.decisionHeuristic: Prefer cross-functional flow for product work, but keep specialist concentration where depth is genuinely scarce and valuable.
entries.whatThisIsReallyAbout: Flow efficiency, domain depth, handoff cost, and how work actually moves through the organization.
entries.notActuallyAbout: Whether one team topology is more progressive than the other.
entries.whyItFeelsHard: Cross-functional teams promise flow; specialist teams promise depth. Both can fail if the work shape does not match.

optionA.name: Specialist Teams
bestWhen: work requires strong concentration of skill
hiddenCosts: teams may optimize locally instead of systemically
dependencies can become structural
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates local excellence with global delivery friction.
realWorldFits: specialist QA, ML, or platform disciplines with scarce expertise

optionB.name: Cross-Functional Teams
hiddenCosts: not all work packages cleanly into vertical ownership
cross-functional in name can still hide specialist bottlenecks
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates teams that appear autonomous but still depend on invisible specialists.
realWorldFits: teams where handoff reduction is a major strategic gain

questionsToAsk: Where does work actually wait today?
Which expertise is too scarce to distribute evenly?
Can a team truly own a vertical slice without hidden dependencies?
Are handoffs or uneven expertise hurting us more?
entries.defaultBias: Prefer cross-functional teams for product flow, but retain specialist structures where expertise concentration is genuinely necessary.
commonBadReasons: everyone should be full-stack in the same way
antiPatterns: calling teams cross-functional when they still queue on the same specialists
keeping specialists centralized after handoff cost clearly dominates

timeHorizonNotes.optionA: Wins where specialist depth remains the limiting factor.
timeHorizonNotes.optionB: Wins where flow and ownership produce more leverage than concentrated expertise.

aiCanHelpWith: AI can help broaden baseline capability, documentation, and knowledge transfer across teams.
aiCanMakeWorseBy: AI may make shallow generalism look stronger than it is, masking where deep expertise still matters.
entries.aiFalseConfidence: AI can accelerate handoffs and summaries between specialist teams, making the hand-off cost look lower than it is — creating the illusion of cross-team collaboration when what improved is the paperwork, not the shared context.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: AI assistance does not remove the need for real specialist judgment in high-risk domains.

easilyConfusedWith.distinction: That decision is about who's accountable for an area. This one is about how teams are composed.
That decision is specifically about platform teams' relationship to product teams. This one is about team composition across the org.
Hiring profiles affect who's available. This decision is how those people are grouped into delivery units.

entries.title: Strong Ownership vs Shared Ownership
entries.decisionQuestion: Should one team clearly own this area, or should multiple teams share ownership?
entries.summary: Usually an accountability-vs-flexibility decision.
entries.decisionHeuristic: Prefer explicit ownership, then design collaboration around it.
entries.whatThisIsReallyAbout: Whether clarity of responsibility matters more than broad access and collective stewardship.
entries.notActuallyAbout: Whether collaboration sounds nicer than accountability.
entries.whyItFeelsHard: Strong ownership improves accountability; shared ownership can improve resilience and coverage if it is real and structured.

optionA.name: Strong Ownership
hiddenCosts: ownership can harden into gatekeeping
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates hero teams or territorial behavior.

optionB.name: Shared Ownership
bestWhen: surface is broad but not deeply bounded
hiddenCosts: shared ownership often means unclear ownership in practice
failureModesWhenMisused: Leads to ownership drift.
realWorldFits: cross-cutting standards maintained by several groups

questionsToAsk: Who is ultimately responsible when this breaks?
Is shared ownership real stewardship or just permission to touch?
Would clearer ownership reduce confusion?
What is the risk of concentration versus diffusion?
entries.defaultBias: Prefer explicit ownership, then design collaboration around it.
antiPatterns: everyone can change it, nobody can answer for it
one owner in theory, many hidden owners in practice
goodSignalsForB: genuine multi-team stewardship with clear rules

optionB: all adjacent teams through coordination drag

timeHorizonNotes.optionA: Usually wins long-term because clarity compounds.
timeHorizonNotes.optionB: Wins only where shared stewardship is truly structured and lightweight.

aiCanHelpWith: AI can help document ownership maps and stale areas.
aiCanMakeWorseBy: AI can make shared code contribution easier while leaving responsibility unclear.
entries.aiFalseConfidence: AI makes shared contribution easier — anyone can generate a viable-looking change to anything — which inflates contribution activity while leaving responsibility for the change unclear.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: More contributors does not mean more ownership clarity.

easilyConfusedWith.distinction: That decision is about team composition. This one is about who's accountable for an area regardless of which team touches it.
That decision is about authority across teams. This one is about ownership within the work itself.
Review policy is about quality gates. This decision is about who carries the consequences of the code after it merges.

entries.title: Local Team Autonomy vs Central Governance
entries.decisionQuestion: How much freedom should teams have versus centrally enforced standards and controls?
entries.summary: Usually a flow-vs-coherence decision.
entries.decisionHeuristic: Govern the few things that truly matter; leave the rest to teams.
entries.whatThisIsReallyAbout: How much variation the organization can tolerate in exchange for local speed and ownership.
entries.notActuallyAbout: Whether freedom or standardization is morally better.
entries.whyItFeelsHard: Autonomy improves local speed; governance protects system coherence and risk control.

optionA.name: Local Autonomy
hiddenCosts: local wins can increase system-wide pain
failureModesWhenMisused: Leads to local optimization and fragmented practices.
realWorldFits: mature product teams with low shared dependency

optionB.name: Central Governance
costs: friction if governance is distant from reality
hiddenCosts: governance can become performance theater
failureModesWhenMisused: Leads to ticket theater and stakeholder frustration without better outcomes.
realWorldFits: shared platform rules that prevent real cross-team harm

questionsToAsk: Which standards prevent real harm versus merely create consistency theater?
What variation can we safely afford?
Are teams mature enough to use autonomy well?
Is governance helping outcomes or producing meetings?
entries.defaultBias: Govern the few things that truly matter; leave the rest to teams.
antiPatterns: governing everything because it feels safer
granting autonomy without clarifying non-negotiables

timeHorizonNotes.optionA: Wins when local context truly dominates and teams are mature.
timeHorizonNotes.optionB: Wins where variation cost compounds faster than local speed gains.

aiCanHelpWith: AI can help codify governance as guidance and checks rather than meetings.
aiCanMakeWorseBy: AI can accelerate local divergence by making alternative implementations cheap.
entries.aiFalseConfidence: Cheap AI-generated local variants make autonomy feel lower-risk — each team's alternative implementation looks small and well-made, hiding the compounding cost of lost coherence at the org level.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: Cheap local variation increases the importance of knowing which standards are non-negotiable.

easilyConfusedWith.distinction: That decision is about accountability for an area. This one is about authority to set direction across areas.
That decision is about product/system variance. This one is about the team authority model that produces or prevents it.
Policy enforcement is the mechanism. This decision is the authority model underneath the mechanism.

entries.title: Documentation First vs Tacit Coordination
entries.decisionQuestion: Should teams rely primarily on written artifacts or on synchronous or tacit coordination?
entries.summary: Usually a scale-and-memory decision.
entries.decisionHeuristic: Document enduring decisions and critical operations; keep ephemeral coordination lightweight.
entries.whatThisIsReallyAbout: How much of the system of work should survive beyond people's heads and meetings.
entries.notActuallyAbout: Whether documentation is virtuous in itself.
entries.whyItFeelsHard: Tacit coordination is fast; documentation is slower but scales and survives.

optionA.name: Documentation First
hiddenCosts: documentation can become ritual rather than useful memory
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates artifact theater with weak real alignment.

optionB.name: Tacit Coordination
hiddenCosts: knowledge lives in people until the cost becomes visible
failureModesWhenMisused: Leads to hero trap and ownership drift.
realWorldFits: rapid problem-solving loops where durability is not yet needed

questionsToAsk: Will someone need this decision or process later?
What happens if the people currently coordinating disappear for two weeks?
Is this worth writing down, or only worth resolving quickly?
Do we trust our docs enough to act on them?
entries.defaultBias: Document enduring decisions and critical operations; keep ephemeral coordination lightweight.
antiPatterns: writing documents nobody uses
keeping critical operational knowledge only in chats and memory

timeHorizonNotes.optionA: Wins as team scale and memory needs increase.
timeHorizonNotes.optionB: Wins only while coordination remains local and short-lived.

aiCanHelpWith: AI can summarize decisions and improve findability of useful docs.
aiCanMakeWorseBy: AI can generate lots of documentation quickly, increasing the risk of stale but polished memory.
entries.aiFalseConfidence: AI makes documents faster to produce, so the org can appear well-documented while the docs are stale, ownerless, and disconnected from real decisions — polished memory that isn't memory.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: Generated docs are only useful if tied to real decisions and maintained ownership.

easilyConfusedWith.distinction: That decision is about real-time vs async collaboration. This one is about whether written artifacts or tacit context is the durable carrier of org memory.
That decision is about who carries accountability. This one is about which artifact carries organizational memory.
Tooling is the substrate. This decision is whether writing-down-first is the coordination mode, regardless of tool.

entries.title: Synchronous Collaboration vs Async-First Collaboration
entries.decisionQuestion: Should the team coordinate mainly through real-time meetings or chat, or asynchronous artifacts and workflows?
entries.summary: Usually a speed-of-alignment vs depth-of-focus decision.
entries.decisionHeuristic: Use async for durable coordination; use sync intentionally for ambiguity and fast convergence.
entries.whatThisIsReallyAbout: Whether communication optimizes for immediate alignment or sustainable scale and focus.
entries.notActuallyAbout: Whether meetings are always bad or writing is always better.
entries.whyItFeelsHard: Sync is faster in the moment; async scales better but requires stronger clarity.

optionA.name: Synchronous Collaboration
hiddenCosts: oral decisions can evaporate
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates meeting culture rot and coordination dependence.

optionB.name: Async-First
costs: misunderstanding risk if writing is weak
hiddenCosts: teams may confuse async with low responsiveness
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates slow drift and unresolved ambiguity under a banner of focus.
realWorldFits: status and planning communication that benefits from durable records

questionsToAsk: Is the issue ambiguous enough to need live discussion?
Does this decision need durable memory?
Is sync solving complexity, or just bypassing weak writing?
Can the team afford the interruption cost?
entries.defaultBias: Use async by default for durable coordination; use sync intentionally for ambiguity and rapid convergence.
antiPatterns: using meetings as a substitute for clarity
using async for issues that clearly need live convergence

optionB: people resolving ambiguous issues more slowly

timeHorizonNotes.optionA: Wins in short bursts when ambiguity is the real bottleneck.
timeHorizonNotes.optionB: Wins long-term by preserving focus and building organizational memory.

aiCanHelpWith: AI can summarize threads and reduce sync load when signal is already present.
aiCanMakeWorseBy: AI can make async artifacts easier to produce but may also increase low-signal communication volume.
entries.aiFalseConfidence: AI-generated summaries make async communication feel sharper, which can mask that the decision itself was never made — the system polishes the output of alignment without creating alignment.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: Cheaper summaries do not remove the need for real decision clarity.

easilyConfusedWith.distinction: That decision is about durable written artifacts. This one is about real-time presence vs deferred artifacts in day-to-day coordination.
Remote policy is about where people work. This decision is about how they coordinate, which is related but distinct.
Meeting culture is the manifestation. This decision is the underlying coordination philosophy.

entries.title: Test Pyramid vs Heavy End-to-End
entries.decisionQuestion: Should confidence rely mainly on lower-level tests or on broad end-to-end coverage?
entries.summary: Usually a feedback-speed vs system-confidence decision.
entries.decisionHeuristic: Bias toward fast lower-level tests, but keep enough end-to-end coverage to prove real flows.
entries.whatThisIsReallyAbout: Where confidence should be created, where it should be verified, and how much slowness the team can afford.
entries.notActuallyAbout: Whether one layer of testing is morally superior.
entries.whyItFeelsHard: Lower-level tests are faster and more precise; end-to-end tests better represent real paths but are slower and flakier.

optionA.name: Test Pyramid
costs: requires discipline in design and testability
can miss integration reality if over-relied on
hiddenCosts: teams can claim confidence without enough system-level truth
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates false confidence in isolated correctness.
realWorldFits: teams optimizing for fast developer feedback

optionB.name: Heavy End-to-End
bestWhen: system behavior is the main concern
hiddenCosts: teams may become dependent on slow brittle suites
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates delivery drag and fragile confidence mechanisms.
realWorldFits: products where key failure risk lives between components

questionsToAsk: Where do failures actually happen: within components or between them?
How much slow feedback can the team sustain?
Are we testing reality or just accumulating test count?
Is the system designed to be testable at lower levels?
entries.defaultBias: Prefer a pyramid-like balance with enough end-to-end to prove real flows.
antiPatterns: measuring confidence by test count alone
using end-to-end tests as a substitute for good system design

optionB: QA and dev teams debugging flakes

timeHorizonNotes.optionA: Wins when developer feedback speed compounds productivity.
timeHorizonNotes.optionB: Wins only when system confidence from real-path testing is the dominant gap.

aiCanHelpWith: AI can help identify coverage gaps and generate scenario ideas.
aiCanMakeWorseBy: AI can generate lots of low-value tests quickly, inflating counts without confidence.
entries.aiFalseConfidence: Generated tests compile, pass, and raise coverage numbers — creating the illusion of a tested system when many of those tests don't actually distinguish correct from incorrect behavior.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: Generated tests are not automatically meaningful tests.

easilyConfusedWith.distinction: That decision is about how strict the CI gate is. This one is about which kinds of tests make up what's behind the gate.
That decision is about how much judgment to keep manual. This one is about which shape of automated coverage to build.
A coverage target is a percentage. This decision is about the distribution of test types that produces that percentage — and whether the result actually signals confidence.

entries.title: CI Gate Strictness vs Developer Throughput
entries.decisionQuestion: How strict should delivery gates be versus how much friction can developers tolerate?
entries.summary: Usually a trust-in-automation vs speed-of-iteration decision.
entries.decisionHeuristic: Be strict on high-risk paths and lighter where reversibility is strong.
entries.whatThisIsReallyAbout: How much risk should be caught before merge or release versus after.
entries.notActuallyAbout: Whether strictness equals quality by itself.
entries.whyItFeelsHard: Stricter gates improve quality control but slow people down; weak gates speed flow but leak risk forward.

optionA.name: Strict Gates
bestWhen: pipeline quality is good enough to earn trust
hiddenCosts: teams may optimize around the gate rather than the intent
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates ritualized compliance and slow flow.

optionB.name: Higher Throughput
hiddenCosts: quality debt can quietly relocate downstream
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates fast merges with slow recovery.

questionsToAsk: What does a failure in this path cost?
Do developers trust the pipeline as signal or noise?
Can we roll back cheaply if a looser gate misses something?
Are we gating risk or just gating activity?
entries.defaultBias: Be strict on high-risk paths and lighter where reversibility is strong.
commonBadReasons: more checks always mean more quality
antiPatterns: requiring slow gates that do not catch meaningful issues
removing gates without strengthening rollback or monitoring

timeHorizonNotes.optionA: Wins when quality escapes are structurally expensive.
timeHorizonNotes.optionB: Wins where fast learning matters and rollback plus monitoring are strong.

aiCanHelpWith: AI can help classify change risk and tune gates by surface.
aiCanMakeWorseBy: AI can increase change volume, which makes weak gates riskier and bad gates more painful.
entries.aiFalseConfidence: AI raises change volume without any corresponding increase in gate quality — a gate that passes 95% of AI-generated PRs cleanly can look effective while catching less than it used to relative to the flow.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: Higher output volume increases need for smart gating, not blanket friction.

easilyConfusedWith.distinction: That decision is about the shape of automated tests. This one is about how strictly those tests are enforced as gates.
