That decision is about where confidence comes from. This one is about how strictly the automated half is applied.
That decision is about feature-level hardening timing. This one is about the specific friction developers feel on every change.

entries.title: Feature Flags vs Branch-Based Release Isolation
entries.decisionQuestion: Should release control happen through runtime flags or through branching and release isolation?
entries.summary: Usually a rollout-control vs codebase-complexity decision.
entries.decisionHeuristic: Use flags for controlled rollout if flag hygiene is strong; avoid both long-lived flags and long-lived branches.
entries.whatThisIsReallyAbout: How to decouple deployment from release while keeping code complexity manageable.
entries.notActuallyAbout: Which approach is more DevOps-pure.
entries.whyItFeelsHard: Flags offer flexibility; branches feel cleaner in the code but slower and riskier to integrate.

optionA.name: Feature Flags
bestWhen: deploy and release decoupling is valuable
hiddenCosts: stale flags accumulate and distort system logic
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates permanent branching logic and operational confusion.
realWorldFits: A/B experimentation and controlled feature exposure

optionB.name: Branch-Based Isolation
hiddenCosts: release branches can become hiding places for integration debt
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates painful reintegration and late discovery of conflicts.
realWorldFits: small teams with low need for progressive exposure
cases where runtime toggle complexity would be worse than temporary branch isolation

questionsToAsk: Do we need progressive exposure or just temporary isolation?
Who owns flag cleanup?
How much merge pain can we tolerate?
What happens when a temporary mechanism becomes permanent?
entries.defaultBias: Prefer flags for controlled rollout if flag hygiene is strong; avoid both long-lived flags and long-lived branches.
antiPatterns: keeping stale flags forever
using long-lived release branches as a substitute for integration discipline

optionA: operations if flag state is unclear

timeHorizonNotes.optionA: Wins when safe rollout and learning matter repeatedly.
timeHorizonNotes.optionB: Wins in narrow cases where isolation is brief and rollout sophistication is unnecessary.

aiCanHelpWith: AI can detect stale flags and branch hotspots.
aiCanMakeWorseBy: AI can generate more flag-dependent code quickly, worsening toggle debt.
entries.aiFalseConfidence: AI generates flag-dependent code easily, so a feature-flag rollout architecture looks lightweight while toggle debt accumulates silently — each flag generated cleanly, the fleet of flags a growing runtime complexity.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: Cheap branching logic becomes expensive runtime complexity.

easilyConfusedWith.distinction: That decision is about reversibility of a change. This one is about the mechanism used to stage rollouts.
That decision is about system change willingness. This one is about release-time control structure.
Blue/green vs canary is about deployment shape. This decision is about how features are exposed once deployed.

entries.title: Backward Compatibility vs Faster Evolution
entries.decisionQuestion: Should we preserve compatibility longer or evolve faster and accept breaking-change pressure?
entries.summary: Usually a consumer-protection vs platform-evolution decision.
entries.decisionHeuristic: Preserve compatibility where consumer trust is strategic; evolve faster only where blast radius is controlled.
entries.whatThisIsReallyAbout: How much existing adoption should constrain future change.
entries.notActuallyAbout: Whether breaking changes prove technical courage.
entries.whyItFeelsHard: Compatibility preserves trust; evolution preserves momentum and simplification.

optionA.name: Backward Compatibility
bestWhen: trust in platform or API matters strongly
hiddenCosts: compatibility can preserve bad design indefinitely
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates long-lived drag through accumulated compatibility obligations.
realWorldFits: platform interfaces with many consuming teams
SDKs and contracts where upgrade coordination is expensive

optionB.name: Faster Evolution
hiddenCosts: teams may externalize their cleanup cost onto others
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates fast-moving producers and exhausted consumers.
realWorldFits: products where the owning org controls all clients

questionsToAsk: Who bears the migration cost?
How many consumers do we truly control?
Is compatibility protecting real trust or preserving bad design?
Can we deprecate cleanly rather than choose all-or-nothing?
entries.defaultBias: Preserve compatibility where consumer trust is strategic; evolve faster only where blast radius is controlled.
antiPatterns: breaking interfaces without migration support
keeping broken legacy patterns forever because deprecation feels hard

timeHorizonNotes.optionA: Wins where trust and adoption ecosystem matter more than cleanup speed.
timeHorizonNotes.optionB: Wins where the producer truly controls migration and simplification gains are meaningful.

aiCanHelpWith: AI can help analyze usage and generate migration guides.
aiCanMakeWorseBy: AI can accelerate interface changes, increasing risk of silent incompatibility if governance is weak.
entries.aiFalseConfidence: AI accelerates interface changes on both sides, making breaking changes feel lower-cost because the consumer migrations can be generated — creating the illusion that compatibility work has been done when it's only been sketched.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: Faster code changes increase compatibility governance need.

easilyConfusedWith.distinction: That decision is about API style. This one is about evolution policy within whichever style you're in.
That decision is about product variance. This one is about interface stability over time.
Versioning policy is the mechanism. This decision is the stance the policy implements.

entries.title: Convention over Configuration vs Flexibility
entries.decisionQuestion: Should the system prefer opinionated defaults or expose more configuration flexibility?
entries.summary: Usually a simplicity-vs-variance decision.
entries.decisionHeuristic: Prefer strong conventions with carefully justified escape hatches.
entries.whatThisIsReallyAbout: How much optionality users or teams really need versus how much complexity that optionality introduces.
entries.notActuallyAbout: Whether more options automatically means a more capable system.
entries.whyItFeelsHard: Conventions simplify the common path; flexibility accommodates edge cases and future diversity.

optionA.name: Convention over Configuration
hiddenCosts: teams may work around rigid defaults in unofficial ways
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates covert customization outside the official model.
realWorldFits: developer tooling with strong paved roads
products where supportability matters more than rare edge-case freedom

optionB.name: Flexibility
bestWhen: opinionated default would be too constraining
hiddenCosts: config surfaces can become a product of their own
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates complexity through optionality few users actually need.
realWorldFits: systems where controlled variation is part of the value proposition

questionsToAsk: Is this option needed often or only imaginable?
What support burden does each configuration option create?
Can we solve this with defaults plus escape hatches instead of broad flexibility?
Who actually benefits from each extra knob?
entries.defaultBias: Prefer strong conventions with carefully justified escape hatches.
antiPatterns: adding config options for hypothetical future needs
forcing rigid conventions that drive unofficial workaround ecosystems

timeHorizonNotes.optionA: Usually wins through lower complexity and stronger defaults.
timeHorizonNotes.optionB: Wins when real variance persists and is worth supporting explicitly.

aiCanHelpWith: AI can analyze config usage to find dead or valuable variation.
aiCanMakeWorseBy: AI can cheaply add configuration surfaces, inflating optionality without real demand.
entries.aiFalseConfidence: AI can cheaply add configuration surfaces, so flexibility appears low-cost — each new option compiles cleanly and looks well-scoped, hiding the aggregate decision fatigue and surface-area cost users and operators absorb.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: Generated options are still product complexity.

easilyConfusedWith.distinction: That decision is about whether a solution covers multiple cases. This one is about how much the solution's user can tune it within one case.
That decision is about variance across instances. This one is about configurability within one instance.
Good defaults are the implementation. This decision is the philosophy that determines whether defaults are strong or just present.

entries.title: Manual Review Depth vs Automation Dependence
entries.decisionQuestion: How much confidence should come from human judgment versus automated checks?
entries.summary: Usually a human-context vs scalable-consistency decision.
entries.decisionHeuristic: Automate the obvious; reserve human depth for what actually needs judgment.
entries.whatThisIsReallyAbout: Which classes of defects and design issues need human reasoning and which can be reliably automated.
entries.notActuallyAbout: Whether humans or tools are more virtuous or modern.
entries.whyItFeelsHard: Humans catch nuance; automation scales. Overreliance on either leaves blind spots.

optionA.name: Manual Review Depth
bestWhen: changes are high-risk or conceptually subtle
automation cannot express the real concern
hiddenCosts: manual review can become rubber-stamp or politics-shaped
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates process drag and uneven quality.
realWorldFits: high-impact behavior changes where context matters

optionB.name: Automation Dependence
costs: false confidence if checks are shallow
hiddenCosts: teams may stop asking questions automation cannot ask
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates approval-shaped quality with conceptual blind spots.
realWorldFits: repeatable static and dynamic quality checks

questionsToAsk: What exactly are humans catching that automation cannot?
What exactly are we asking humans to do that automation should already do?
Which changes need judgment rather than validation?
Is review still understanding-shaped, or only approval-shaped?
entries.defaultBias: Automate the obvious; reserve human depth for what actually needs judgment.
antiPatterns: asking humans to repeat mechanical checks
trusting automation on design questions it cannot assess

timeHorizonNotes.optionA: Wins where contextual judgment prevents expensive errors.
timeHorizonNotes.optionB: Wins wherever repeatable consistency matters and the checks are genuinely good.

aiCanHelpWith: AI can assist reviewers by summarizing diffs and likely hotspots.
aiCanMakeWorseBy: AI increases output volume, making weak human review and weak automation both more dangerous.
entries.aiFalseConfidence: AI-assisted review suggestions look thoughtful because they include explanations and cite policy — creating the illusion of reviewed work when the reviewer approved a generated summary, not the change itself.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: AI-assisted review is not the same as real review depth.

easilyConfusedWith.distinction: That decision is about automated test shape. This one is about whether automation or human judgment is the primary source of confidence.
That decision is about development. This one is about what happens after development — specifically who or what verifies the outcome.
That decision is about the gate's strictness. This one is about whether the gate relies on humans or machines.

entries.title: AI-Assisted Development vs Manual-Only Development
entries.decisionQuestion: How much of the engineering workflow should rely on AI assistance?
entries.summary: Usually a judgment-and-verification decision, not a productivity ideology decision.
entries.decisionHeuristic: Use AI assistance selectively where verification is strong and ownership remains clear.
entries.whatThisIsReallyAbout: Where generation is safe, where understanding is essential, and how review practices must change.
entries.notActuallyAbout: Whether AI use automatically means modernity or whether manual work is automatically safer.
entries.whyItFeelsHard: AI clearly helps in some work, but weak controls turn speed into synthetic velocity.

optionA.name: AI-Assisted Development
hiddenCosts: teams may confuse generation speed with durable progress
style consistency can hide conceptual inconsistency
failureModesWhenMisused: Leads to synthetic velocity and autocomplete architecture.
realWorldFits: documentation, migration helpers, and repetitive code tasks under strong review

optionB.name: Manual-Only Development
bestWhen: team lacks safe AI workflow controls
hiddenCosts: teams may become ideologically anti-tool rather than risk-aware
manual work can waste senior attention
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates unnecessary friction and loses competitive productivity where safe leverage exists.
realWorldFits: workflows where explainability and authorship must stay direct

questionsToAsk: Can we verify this work strongly enough if AI helps produce it?
Who will own and explain the output later?
Does AI reduce toil here or blur understanding?
What categories of work should remain human-heavy by design?
entries.defaultBias: Use AI assistance selectively where verification is strong and ownership remains clear.
commonBadReasons: AI should never touch production work
antiPatterns: treating generated code volume as progress
allowing reviewers to approve code they do not understand because AI produced it

optionA: future maintainers if understanding is weak

timeHorizonNotes.optionA: Wins when leverage compounds without eroding understanding.
timeHorizonNotes.optionB: Wins only where risk and explainability requirements truly outweigh the missed leverage.

aiCanHelpWith: It reduces toil in low-risk repetitive work and accelerates exploration when well governed.
aiCanMakeWorseBy: AI-native by definition: it can accelerate both leverage and error propagation.
entries.aiFalseConfidence: The AI-assisted path produces code that compiles and passes surface checks regardless of whether the author understands what they shipped — the safety question 'do we still know what we wrote?' is invisible at the moment of shipping.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: The unit of safety is not whether AI was used; it is whether understanding and verification are still sufficient.

easilyConfusedWith.distinction: That decision is about verification. This one is about the authoring phase itself.
That decision is about org-level AI policy. This one is about the engineer-level workflow within whatever policy is in place.
That decision is about who gets access. This one is about whether any given engineer uses AI in their day-to-day work.

entries.title: Model API vs Self-Hosted Model
entries.decisionQuestion: Should we rely on external model APIs or host models ourselves?
entries.summary: Usually a control-vs-complexity decision.
entries.decisionHeuristic: Prefer APIs unless control, privacy, latency, or economics clearly justify self-hosting.
entries.whatThisIsReallyAbout: Latency, cost, governance, privacy, reliability, and operational capability.
entries.notActuallyAbout: Whether self-hosting sounds more advanced or strategically serious.
entries.whyItFeelsHard: APIs are fast to adopt; self-hosting offers control but adds major operational responsibility.

optionA.name: Model API
bestWhen: model quality matters more than control
hiddenCosts: provider changes can surface as silent model drift
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates operational dependence on a provider without enough evaluation guardrails.
realWorldFits: use cases where external provider behavior is acceptable

optionB.name: Self-Hosted Model
bestWhen: privacy, governance, or control requirements are strong
hiddenCosts: self-hosting can distract teams from product value
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates an AI infrastructure program where a product decision was needed.
realWorldFits: high-volume internal AI surfaces with stable usage
organizations with real model operations capability

questionsToAsk: What exactly do we gain from hosting ourselves?
Do we have real serving capability or only enthusiasm?
How sensitive is the data and where does that matter legally or operationally?
What happens if provider pricing or behavior changes?
entries.defaultBias: Prefer API unless control, privacy, latency, or economics clearly justify self-hosting.
commonBadReasons: APIs are too risky by definition
antiPatterns: self-hosting for prestige
using APIs without evaluation guardrails because setup was fast

optionA: teams exposed to provider behavior changes
optionB: product teams delayed by infra burden

timeHorizonNotes.optionA: Usually wins early through speed and lower ops overhead.
timeHorizonNotes.optionB: Wins only when control or economics sustain the ongoing infrastructure burden.

aiCanHelpWith: AI can help benchmark request patterns and cost profiles across both choices.
aiCanMakeWorseBy: AI hype can distort both directions: APIs seem magically easy, self-hosting seems strategically superior.
entries.aiFalseConfidence: APIs look strategically fragile and self-hosting looks strategically strong regardless of the actual workload — the narrative is loud on both sides and can turn an infrastructure decision into an identity commitment.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: Do not turn infrastructure ownership into identity.

easilyConfusedWith.distinction: That decision is about how knowledge gets to the model. This one is about where the model runs.
That decision is about org readiness. This one is about infrastructure shape, which may or may not be ready for either path.
Picking an API vendor is a buying choice. This decision is whether you're in the buy lane at all for the model itself.

entries.title: RAG vs Fine-Tuning
entries.decisionQuestion: Should this AI capability rely on retrieval-augmented generation or model fine-tuning?
entries.summary: Usually a knowledge-grounding vs behavior-shaping decision.
entries.decisionHeuristic: Use RAG when knowledge changes; use fine-tuning when behavior must change.
entries.whatThisIsReallyAbout: Where truth lives, what needs to change, and whether the core problem is missing knowledge or missing learned behavior.
entries.notActuallyAbout: Which technique is more advanced.
entries.whyItFeelsHard: Both are often discussed as generic upgrades, but they solve different classes of problems.

optionA.name: RAG
bestWhen: behavior is acceptable but context is missing
strengths: less need to retrain for content change
hiddenCosts: weak corpus quality poisons the whole system
retrieval confidence can look better than truth quality
failureModesWhenMisused: Leads to RAG without ground truth.
realWorldFits: internal enterprise search and answer surfaces
support systems grounded in living documentation

optionB.name: Fine-Tuning
bestWhen: behavior style or patterning must change
domain behavior matters more than changing knowledge
hiddenCosts: teams may try to tune behavior to compensate for bad data or weak grounding
evaluation complexity rises quickly
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates specialized behavior without trustworthy truth sources or robust evaluation.

questionsToAsk: Is the problem missing knowledge or missing behavior?
How often does the source truth change?
Do we need citations and source trust?
Can we evaluate behavior quality well enough to justify tuning?
entries.defaultBias: Prefer RAG for changing knowledge and grounding; use fine-tuning when the problem is behavior, not missing context.
commonBadReasons: one approach should solve both knowledge and behavior problems
antiPatterns: using fine-tuning to hide bad grounding
using RAG to solve a behavior problem
goodSignalsForB: behavior shift matters more than knowledge freshness

optionB: product teams if tuning cycles are slow

timeHorizonNotes.optionA: Wins when source truth keeps changing and grounding matters.
timeHorizonNotes.optionB: Wins when task behavior is stable enough to justify training investment.

aiCanHelpWith: AI can help synthesize eval sets and compare failure patterns across both approaches.
aiCanMakeWorseBy: This is an AI-native decision; hype around both options distorts judgment fast.
entries.aiFalseConfidence: Outputs from both options read as fluent and confident regardless of whether retrieval surfaced the right sources or the model learned the right behavior — creating the illusion of a working system long before grounding and behavior have actually been verified.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: Do not use fine-tuning to hide grounding problems, and do not use RAG to solve behavior problems.

easilyConfusedWith.distinction: That decision is about where the model runs. This one is about how to shape the knowledge the model brings to the task.
That decision is about how to verify the answer. This one is the architectural choice whose quality that evaluation is trying to measure.
Prompt engineering is how you ask a model for an answer. RAG vs fine-tuning is how the relevant knowledge gets to the model in the first place.

entries.title: Prompt Layer vs Workflow/Tooling Layer
entries.decisionQuestion: Should system quality depend mainly on prompt engineering or on stronger orchestration, tools, and workflow structure?
entries.summary: Usually a wording-vs-system-design decision.
entries.decisionHeuristic: Use prompts for local behavior tuning; move to workflow and tooling when process structure matters.
entries.whatThisIsReallyAbout: Whether the problem is prompt behavior or missing system scaffolding and tools.
entries.notActuallyAbout: Who is better at prompting.
entries.whyItFeelsHard: Prompts are easy to change; workflow and tooling are more durable but costlier to design.

optionA.name: Prompt Layer
bestWhen: behavior can improve through instruction tuning
hiddenCosts: teams may compensate for workflow gaps with brittle prompting
failureModesWhenMisused: Leads to prompt ops chaos.
realWorldFits: early experimentation where tooling is not yet the bottleneck

optionB.name: Workflow/Tooling Layer
bestWhen: task requires state, tools, or multi-step structure
quality depends on process, not just phrasing
hiddenCosts: tooling complexity can grow past value if overbuilt
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates overengineered workflows for problems that were mostly prompt-level.

questionsToAsk: Is the problem really wording, or is it process and state?
What fails repeatedly that prompt changes have not fixed?
Do we need tools, memory, or orchestration?
Can we observe the workflow well enough to own it?
entries.defaultBias: Use prompts for local behavior tuning; move to workflow and tooling when process structure matters.
commonBadReasons: everything can be fixed with better prompting
all AI systems need orchestration stacks
antiPatterns: growing prompts indefinitely instead of fixing system design
building orchestration layers for a problem that is still single-step

timeHorizonNotes.optionA: Wins early for small, bounded problems.
timeHorizonNotes.optionB: Wins when the system must behave reliably across multi-step tasks and real-world variability.

aiCanHelpWith: AI can help compare prompt variants and workflow bottlenecks.
aiCanMakeWorseBy: Teams can overfit prompts quickly and mistake local improvements for system quality.
entries.aiFalseConfidence: Incremental prompt improvements produce visible local wins on the eval you happened to run, creating the illusion of system-level progress — when what actually changed was a local fit, not a durable capability.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: Prompt quality cannot compensate indefinitely for missing system structure.

easilyConfusedWith.distinction: That decision is about how the model gets domain knowledge. This one is about where quality is engineered — prompts or orchestration.
That decision is about workflow autonomy. This one is about what carries the workflow's quality.
Prompt engineering is an activity. This decision is whether that activity is the right load-bearing investment.

entries.title: Human-in-the-Loop vs Full Automation
entries.decisionQuestion: Should this workflow require human review or intervention, or run autonomously end to end?
entries.summary: Usually a trust-boundary and consequence-of-error decision.
entries.decisionHeuristic: Keep humans in the loop until task quality is genuinely proven and consequence is bounded.
entries.whatThisIsReallyAbout: Where human judgment is still required and what the true cost of wrong autonomous behavior is.
entries.notActuallyAbout: Whether full automation is more impressive.
entries.whyItFeelsHard: Automation promises scale; human review preserves control but reduces throughput.

optionA.name: Human-in-the-Loop
hiddenCosts: humans can become rubber stamps if workflow design is weak
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates expensive manual approval theater without real judgment value.
realWorldFits: content moderation and approval workflows with real consequence

optionB.name: Full Automation
bestWhen: error consequence is low or tightly bounded
costs: greater need for strong evaluation and monitoring
hiddenCosts: trust can collapse quickly if autonomy outruns reliability
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates confident automated mistakes at scale.
realWorldFits: high-volume repetitive actions with strong monitoring

questionsToAsk: What is the cost of a wrong automated decision?
Is the human review real judgment or just a click-through step?
Can we detect and recover from autonomous failure quickly?
What evidence proves the workflow is ready for autonomy?
entries.defaultBias: Keep humans in the loop until task quality is genuinely proven and consequence is bounded.
commonBadReasons: human review always makes systems safer
antiPatterns: calling rubber-stamp review human oversight
automating high-consequence workflows on benchmark optimism alone

optionB: users and support teams when automation is wrong

timeHorizonNotes.optionA: Wins while trust is still being earned and judgment remains expensive to encode.
timeHorizonNotes.optionB: Wins when the workflow is stable enough that scale matters more than human caution.

aiCanHelpWith: AI can help triage where human review is actually needed most.
aiCanMakeWorseBy: AI systems can appear more capable than they are, pushing premature automation.
entries.aiFalseConfidence: A system looks capable because it produces fluent, correct-shaped outputs on the happy path — creating pressure toward full automation long before the system has been evaluated on the cases where human judgment actually matters.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: Human presence is only valuable if the workflow preserves real judgment.

easilyConfusedWith.distinction: That decision is about how to evaluate model quality. This one is about whether the workflow relies on human oversight at runtime.
That decision is about org-level pacing. This one is about specific workflow risk posture.
A copilot is a UI pattern. This decision is whether authority to act sits with the human or the system.

entries.title: Fast AI Adoption vs Governance-First AI Adoption
entries.decisionQuestion: Should the organization move quickly on AI adoption or establish stronger governance before broad rollout?
entries.summary: Usually a learning-speed vs control-readiness decision.
entries.decisionHeuristic: Move fast in bounded low-risk zones while governance catches up deliberately on high-risk surfaces.
entries.whatThisIsReallyAbout: How much experimentation to allow before policies, controls, and risk models mature.
entries.notActuallyAbout: Whether caution or speed is more visionary.
entries.whyItFeelsHard: Moving fast creates learning; moving carefully protects against misuse and reputational harm.

optionA.name: Fast AI Adoption
bestWhen: use cases are low-risk or sandboxed
governance can evolve alongside limited rollout
hiddenCosts: early norms harden before governance catches up
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates broad unsafe usage patterns that become hard to unwind.

optionB.name: Governance-First
bestWhen: data, legal, or privacy constraints are strong
hiddenCosts: teams may route around governance if it is too slow
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates policy drag and shadow adoption outside the official path.
realWorldFits: externally visible AI surfaces with reputational exposure

questionsToAsk: Which use cases are safe enough to move quickly on now?
Where would weak governance create material harm?
How likely are teams to route around a slow official path?
Can we separate low-risk exploration from high-risk deployment?
entries.defaultBias: Move fast in bounded low-risk zones while governance catches up deliberately on high-risk surfaces.
commonBadReasons: governance will only slow us down
governance must be perfect before anything starts
antiPatterns: rolling out broadly before classifying risk surfaces
creating so much governance drag that shadow AI becomes inevitable

timeHorizonNotes.optionA: Wins by accelerating capability learning, but only in bounded zones.
timeHorizonNotes.optionB: Wins where the cost of a bad early pattern is larger than the value of fast experimentation.

aiCanHelpWith: AI can help classify use cases by risk and sensitivity for staged rollout.
aiCanMakeWorseBy: AI hype inflates pressure toward both reckless speed and performative caution.
entries.aiFalseConfidence: Early visible wins from broad AI use — faster PRs, polished docs, cleaner emails — create the impression of institutional leverage and make governance feel like a drag, hiding the variance in how much of that output is actually understood.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: Governance must be proportionate, not theatrical.

easilyConfusedWith.distinction: That decision is about who has access. This one is about the timing of adoption relative to controls.
That decision is workflow-specific. This one is org-wide pacing.
Policy is the artifact. This decision is the stance the policy encodes.

entries.title: Broad AI Enablement vs Restricted High-Trust AI Use
entries.decisionQuestion: Should AI use be broadly available or limited to high-trust workflows and teams?
entries.summary: Usually a leverage-distribution vs risk-surface decision.
entries.decisionHeuristic: Broaden access gradually as trustworthy usage patterns are proven.
entries.whatThisIsReallyAbout: How widely capability should spread before trust, training, and controls are mature.
entries.notActuallyAbout: Whether access restriction is elitist or broad access is automatically empowering.
entries.whyItFeelsHard: Broad enablement increases leverage; restricted use protects quality and risk boundaries.

optionA.name: Broad AI Enablement
hiddenCosts: bad habits spread quickly
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates broad synthetic velocity and uneven trust.
realWorldFits: internal ideation and low-risk coding assistance
broad knowledge assistant access with safe boundaries

optionB.name: Restricted High-Trust Use
bestWhen: organization wants to learn in controlled zones
hiddenCosts: restricted users may become new hero bottlenecks
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates elite AI islands with weak organizational learning.

questionsToAsk: Which AI uses are low-risk enough for broad access?
Where would poor usage create outsized harm?
Do we have training and norms to support broad rollout?
Would restriction create new bottlenecks or shadow usage?
entries.defaultBias: Broaden access gradually as trustworthy usage patterns are proven.
commonBadReasons: only experts should ever use AI
antiPatterns: broad rollout without safe-use training
restricting access so tightly that learning never diffuses

timeHorizonNotes.optionA: Wins when broad low-risk leverage compounds and governance can keep up.
timeHorizonNotes.optionB: Wins when controlled trust-building matters more than broad experimentation.

aiCanHelpWith: AI can support training and safe-use guidance as rollout widens.
aiCanMakeWorseBy: AI spread multiplies both leverage and inconsistency quickly.
entries.aiFalseConfidence: A broad enablement rollout looks successful by adoption metrics — logins, prompts sent, features used — while inconsistent output quality and uneven review rigor build up unseen across the long tail of use cases.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: Access strategy is not enough; usage quality strategy matters more.

easilyConfusedWith.distinction: That decision is about timing. This one is about scope of access.
That decision is per-engineer workflow. This one is about org-level access shape.
Training is the supporting investment. This decision is whether access is broad in the first place.

entries.title: Synthetic Evaluation vs Real-World Evaluation
entries.decisionQuestion: Should model or system quality be judged mostly through synthetic evals or through real task and production evidence?
entries.summary: Usually a speed-of-measurement vs truth-of-measurement decision.
entries.decisionHeuristic: Use synthetic evals for screening, but anchor major decisions in real task evidence.
entries.whatThisIsReallyAbout: How much confidence synthetic proxies deserve relative to actual user and task behavior.
entries.notActuallyAbout: Which benchmark score looks better in a deck.
entries.whyItFeelsHard: Synthetic evals are cheaper and faster; real-world evals are messier but closer to value.

optionA.name: Synthetic Evaluation
hiddenCosts: teams may optimize to the benchmark instead of the task
failureModesWhenMisused: Leads to benchmark mirage.
realWorldFits: controlled scenario screening before live testing

optionB.name: Real-World Evaluation
bestWhen: task quality matters more than benchmark optics
production or shadow evidence is available
hiddenCosts: real-world signals can be noisy and slow to interpret
failureModesWhenMisused: Creates evaluation lag so long that teams stop learning systematically.
realWorldFits: evaluation of real user workflows and escalation outcomes

questionsToAsk: What does this score actually predict about user value?
Where does the proxy diverge from reality?
Can we collect real task evidence safely?
Are we optimizing the benchmark or the job to be done?
entries.defaultBias: Use synthetic evals for screening, but anchor major decisions in real task evidence.
commonBadReasons: real-world eval is too messy to matter
antiPatterns: shipping based on benchmark optics alone
avoiding real-world evaluation because it complicates the narrative
goodSignalsForB: task quality matters more than benchmark optics

optionA: users and support teams if proxies mislead decisions

timeHorizonNotes.optionA: Wins early for rapid iteration and comparison.
timeHorizonNotes.optionB: Wins whenever real user trust or task quality matters more than eval speed.

aiCanHelpWith: AI can help generate scenario-based eval sets and cluster real-world failures.
aiCanMakeWorseBy: AI systems are especially vulnerable to proxy overfitting.
entries.aiFalseConfidence: Synthetic evals produce crisp repeatable numbers, so the system looks measurable — creating the illusion of progress when the proxy may have decoupled from the thing users actually care about.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: A repeatable proxy can still be the wrong proxy.

Benchmark choice sits inside synthetic eval. This decision is whether synthetic eval is the right trust source at all.
